reasoning: The question asks if the number of protests in India in a 30-day period will be more than 10 times the baseline (1 + the 30-day average over the prior 360 days). ACLED typically records hundreds to thousands of protests per month in India. Achieving a 10x spike over the annual monthly average would require tens of thousands of protests in a single month—a historically unprecedented event. The current reference value of 1.0 indicates exactly 0 protests recorded in the trailing 360 days, purely due to an extended data publishing delay (ACLED's 'Research' tier has a 12-month delay on full event details). Once the data is published and backfilled, the reference baseline will jump to its true historical average (hundreds per month), and the target 30-day periods will also be populated with their true numbers. Thus, it is extremely unlikely that the target periods will ever exceed 10x the backfilled annual average. In the strange edge case where the baseline is permanently left at 0 but new real-time data comes online, a small number of protests would trigger a YES. However, this is unlikely given ACLED's data practices. As required, uncertainty slightly increases with the forecast horizon.
